reviews.review:
I had cmsc330 with Anwar and between him and Hicks/Foster, he's probably the worst professor to take for this class. Anwar is a cool dude, but his lectures were terrible and he moves at a pace that was too fast for most students to catch everything. His explanations are less than clear, and I soon ended up watching Hick's online lectures to try and get the rest of the material.

There were two midterms, fairly tricky but not too bad. Both of them were pretty long, and took the full 75 mins right up to the line - they really pack the exams. Definitely study the exams from previous semesters and drill ocaml/prolog programming if you want to do well on these.

Otherwise, it's actually an awesome class and the material is really interesting despite the workload. I felt pretty solid in my standing as a software eng. after this class, because a lot of concepts across different languages kinda clicked for me. Get Cameron Moy as your TA if you can :) Good luck!

Mamat is a very accommodating professor, but his lectures are completely worthless. On more than one occasion, he would explain the material in the first 45 minutes while barely moving through the slides, and then rapidly go through them, only doing quizzes AND SOMETIMES SKIPPING IMPORTANT THINGS ALTOGETHER! 
Fortunately, if Foster/another professor posts their lectures online, the class will be a breeze. Also, the project descriptions are very easy to understand, and the public tests are usually pretty robust, which is great especially if you're used to Nelson's "figure it out if you can" approach.
I like the guy, but I didn't attend lecture after the first month and still got an A on the final just by reading slides and doing projects. That pretty much sums up how important his lectures are right there.

The lectures were way too fast and not engaging. By the end of the semester, I was skipping class and watching Foster’s lectures online. The tests and projects were fair, although we were left to just figure out basic SQL on the last one, which was annoying. The class itself is an interesting one, and old tests are provided for study. It’s certainly not the end of the world or anything if you have Mamat, you’ll still learn things, just probably not as much as if you had Foster.
what the heck is wrong with this dude. He goes over his lectures really fast. Cannot take notes and cannot understand anything.
Goes over slides pretty quickly, but he is pretty engaging in my opinion. Just sit in the front.

He is extremely engaging and I honestly understood the material so well after. He goes fast but the slides are online so you can just note what he says. I'm a visual learner and he's the best at explaining through code examples that he writes on the spot. I would say he is definitely a better professor to have for 330.

I like anwar and he keeps class fun but the material is just kinda boring so it is hard to pay attention sometimes. The class feels like just memorizing a bunch of java libraries used for multi threading. Easy Class though and projects are not too bad.

He is a horrible lecturer. Do not take him unless you are willing to basically learn all the material on your own outside of class. To make matters worse, he has tons of clicker questions to penalize you for not going to his pointless lectures. And he doesn't allow laptops in class for students who actually want to learn while he's "teaching." The one good thing about his classes is that the grading structure is fair, and the class is organized, but he just has no idea how to communicate the material. He doesn't seem to understand that coding in front of the class in a ridiculously small font while providing little explanation as to what he's doing is not the best way to teach computer science. Going from him one semester to an actually competent lecturer like Eastman the next was a night and day difference.

Anwar is a good choice. You will have to learn some things on your own as he doesn't go through everything fully. The amount of notes he provides for students is AWESOME. His tangents are funny but were less appreciated when we had to speed through topics at the end of the semester.

Anwar is a pretty good lecturer and incredibly funny. His exams are fair, the average my semester was an 80% on the nose. The only valid gripe people have with him is the pacing of his lectures. He does tend to fly through some of the material on the slides, but if you try to pay attention, you'll be fine. Even moreso, all of his lectures are online, and his lectures are recorded and put online in case you missed them, so you should be fine. The projects will be more challenging than the ones you have had so far in 132 and 216 because you will be forced to navigate through different languages and different concepts, but for the most part, you will survive. 

People have gripes with him for having clicker questions that force you to come to lecture, but that really isn't criticism, because you should come to lecture anyway and he's giving you easy points that'll bump your grade at the end of the semester. 

All around a nice professor and person, and I definitely recommend you take 330 with Anwar. I've never had Eastman, but I hear he's ok too.

Funny stories, nice guy.  Class grades are pretty fair.
Projects are kinda butt, and clicker questions suck, lectures are mediocre.  I also hate slide trivia exams (which is what he gives)

Anwar is not a terrible instructor, but not great either. He mostly reads off the slides and often rushes through topics. He tells a lot of stories and goes on a lot of tangents, which would be fine if it didn't mean rushing through challenging topics. I often felt like I didn't learn much by going to lecture and wished I didn't need to go, but there are clicker questions in order to enforce attendance. The material itself is very cool and the TAs for 330 are phenomenal. Also, Hicks is supposedly incredible, so if he's an option, take him. Otherwise, you'll be OK with Anwar, you just might need to put in a little extra time and effort.
Decent lecturer, although he goes a bit fast at times. He is a nice guy and has some funny stories.

Anwar is a great lecturer for 132. He also explains concepts very well. His lectures slides are great too. He doesn't put anything on the exams that he doesn't cover in lecture. Exams are also pretty easy. I usually finish with 20 minutes left and use the rest of the time to check my answers. I also dislike the MC section, which make up a fair portion of the exams, because they're just memorization and can be kind of tricky. Projects are hard to implement, but the project tests aren't hard to pass. I think I got a 100 on most of my projects. When I took it, projects were 40% of the grade, which should make the class an easy A.
Solid guy but not a good lecturer. Reads off slides. Lectures are so useless but attendance is mandatory via clickers. Slides are good.

I had Anwar for 433 and 330. He knows how to run a course well, curves fairly, and is generally pretty responsive to student concerns. I think he is a good standard that other UMD CS professors should hold themselves to. His slides are pretty good and his tests are fair.

Anwar flies through the material very quickly, but that's just because there's a lot of stuff to cover. This semester he managed the online transition due to the pandemic quite well, and he toned down the amount of work we had to do. The slides really are the primary resource in the class, so study those, look at them when doing projects, and you'll do great!
Took this course in the summer and Anwar was a great professor for it. Made class very fun, and made it easy to understand. Plan on taking his 330 class

Not a good lecturer, because he goes super fast over complicated material, and could be a bit more civil, but that's really the only main complaint.  Clicker questions are not the worst and his slides are very comprehensive, the projects are fairly reasonable to do (better than 216's for sure) and the exams were fair too. Nothing completely out of the blue. Some TAs were really really helpful too and were responsive on Piazza. Overall, since 330 is usually co-taught, you should be fine taking either Anwar or the other professor.
He is boring but the content is fine. He doesn't really care much about his students but the TAs are awesome. Start projects the day they are posted and you will be in good shape.

Good professor that knows the material well. Mostly reads off slides but gives alot of examples which helped alot. TAs are very good and office hours provided alot of help on projects. Projects are standard and should be started when they are assigned.

Anwar manages to keep the lecture engaging and entertaining the whole class, he's a great professor and I have no dought I would have received a higher grade had I taken this class in-person. Genuine guy who really cares about his students and offers them a lot of resources and opportunities to ask for help in office hours.

Anwar is fine. He explains the material decent enough in lecture, but he moves so fast! It's really hard to keep up with him during lectures. Also, the exams for CMSC433 are kind of really hard. I aced every single project but I couldn't for the life of me manage better than a 71 on the midterm. I don't expect to do much better on the final. All in all, Anwar is just okay. Not the worst CMSC professor I've ever had but certainly far from the best.

Pretty good 400 class overall. I don't think Anwar is a good lecturer, but he's pretty accommodating by making clicker questions fairly easy and worth 5% of the final grade to redistribute from exams. Some projects were tough (akka), especially due a high amount of the grade being secret tests, but others were fine. Make sure you put time into them, since they're worth a lot, and if you do you should be able to get a good grade. 
I thought the midterm and the final were fine, since I found them to be somewhat modeled after old exams. Also, TAs were great; they were really responsive on Piazza (much more than other 400s) and helpful for office hours. Great slides, and the material itself is also quite useful. Thus, if Anwar is teaching this class, I'd recommend taking it with him.
Had him for CMSC132 and had to take him again because he's great. Would take another class with him if I could.
He's great! Goes very fast but at least for 330, everything you need to know is on the slides anyway.

This professor was very good. He made sure that everything you needed to know was in the lecture slides. Make sure to follow what he is teaching and do the practice that he puts up and you will be fine. The assignments are tough if you do not both to learn/practice the material that he is teaching. As a teacher he seems to genuily enjoy teaching the students and it was a breath of fresh air compared to other professors here.

Not great lecturing style IMO. Lots of "you know?" "this stuff" "this thing" "this stuff is tricky" "just read the JavaDoc". Little motivational content to introduce concepts, which I've always found very useful in CMSC. Absolutely flies through examples without prefacing them well. Much prefer Dr. Hicks or even Prof. Herman hyper-explicit lecturing style and projects.

Projects are open-ended and almost guaranteed to be 75% or more secret tests. TAs are exceptional but projects will still take 15-30 hours for average student, which was hell for someone balancing career responsibilities and other CMSC4xx classes as well. Midterms were fair and lecture slides were good.

Main complaints are lecturing style and project difficulty/disconnect from lecture material/lack of direction given in description.

Do not let this review discourage you from taking 433 with Anwar if you enjoy his style or are simply... well... smarter than me. Anwar is cool but the class just did not resonate with me. I've heard he's pretty good for 330 though.

Second class with Anwar and he's a great professor. His exams are pretty easy for the most part since he emphasizes more on projects (which I 100% agree with). The projects in the class range from extremely easy (Hadoop and MPI) to challenging (Akka), but they're all good skills to know. I didn't attend lecture live as he uploaded the recording, and he does fly through the material, but the slides and GitHub repository provide enough examples to help you understand the material.

I guess Anwar's gone now, but in case he comes back...

I would say he's a good lecturer. My main complaint would be that he never really slows down. We would go through a huge amount of slides everyday and sometimes it seemed like he was racing. 330 has a lot of content, so that's probably why. I don't personally take notes, but I don't think anyone could with how fast he goes. He still did a good job at explaining concepts and the slides were well made, so you could basically just study off of those. He was clearly frustrated this semester by the turnout in lecture. Clicker quizzes used to be 4% of the grade, basically making lecture attendance mandatory. They changed the policy in the first week of the semester I believe, since people could be in and out with COVID. He would make lots of comments to himself during lecture about how it's so hard to teach this semester with not that many people in lecture and with everyone wearing masks. I also guess that computers used to not be allowed in 330 lecture, but now they are? He would complain about people having computers out too sometimes, and how he couldn't do anything.

The projects in this class weren't very difficult, except for the NFA to DFA project and maybe the first OCaml project. If you start early, you should be fine. There were two online midterms which were pretty fair. We had four 20 minute online quizzes in the semester that I found to be very difficult. 20 minutes was not enough time and they seemed a bit unnecessary (the last quiz was on a topic on the last midterm, which was a week before). The final was also in-person for some reason. People would complain on Piazza and the TAs said that the decision was made by the instructors. People tried to ask Mamat/Eastman to change it to in-person and they said they would think about, but they did nothing. Not a good look on their part, considering the online midterms were open book and this wasn't. Plus, many people expressed COVID concerns due to a in-person final, which was also ignored.

All in all, if Anwar comes back, I would recommend him. I was planning on taking 433 with him, but they changed teachers. He seems to have been a bit disillusioned by teaching this semester by all his comments on lecture, but I do hope he comes back.

Anwar is not a good lecturer, the lectures are incredibly disengaging, and trying to take notes is hard as he flies through his slides. He yaps away while doing a poor job explaining new concepts, and if it were not for his in-lecture "clicker" quizzes, I would self-study because at least his online lecture slides at least contain everything he says, not to mention that he records. Cliff's notes are arguably more detailed and understandable though.

I do give him credit for his extremely fair exams and grading, he seems to be generous with partial credit.

Anwar is a great lecturer and funny guy. However, very similar to Nelson in that he annoys some people because he sometimes takes time out of lecture to tell a funny story(something I personally love). This was not ideal since he was coteaching and would sometimes have to speedrun to catch up to Cliff's class but imo it was worth it. 

Early on in the semester it was a little rough because it was his first time teaching after a couple years working, but once he got into it I really learned a lot. Projects were long but usually 2-3 weeks were given for them so if you actually started within a few days of them being assigned you could get them done pretty easily. He also gives some sample code that is very similar to the project. We had either a quiz or exam every two weeks which was really nice because it kept me accountable for the material and studying for any exam wasn't that hard because I only had to study like two weeks of content(assuming I remembered everything).

Anwar was great! He's a pretty funny guy, so I though his lectures were always fairly entertaining, but he does go kinda fast sometimes. He has in class clicker questions, which are a part of your grade, so going to lecture is kinda required. We had 7 projects throughout the semester, most of which I thought were pretty difficult, but if you started early on them, you'd be fine. 2 midterms and a final, which were all pretty easy and similar to the practice exams. We also had 4 discussion quizzes throughout the semester, which were also not that bad. Overall, I definitely recommend taking this class with Anwar!

Don't get me wrong, Anwar seems like a genuinely nice and intelligent guy. That being said, he is one of the worst computer science professors I've had at UMD. CMSC 330 by itself is notoriously disorganized. They seem to change the syllabus and languages covered every semester, never seeming quite certain about what they are going to do over the 15 weeks and jolting around during lectures. Everything is so haphazard and nothing is properly communicated. There are four quizzes during the lecture and they always cover content that was poorly taught during the lectures that happened that week specifically. There is rarely any time given to actually absorb the content before the quizzes. Projects vary, some being hard and others much easier. Most of the projects will have to be self study because they barely cover what we need to know for them during lecture. Anwar himself is a poor lecturer. He zooms through slides and is always flipping between a powerpoint and a terminal or just freeballing and using his web browser. Because of this it never seems like he has a solid idea of what he's trying to cover during the lecture. The resources given to us are also super messy. I feel like 1/3 of the content we need to know is actually taught and the other 2/3 are scattered across random GIT repositories, massive slide decks that are only half covered in class, or other notes. You will feel lost and alone in the course and there is no one really to help you except your friends who are taking it too. The exams oscillated between very fair and very challenging, with some questions seeming near impossible to finish. Cliff doesn't seem much better and they co-teach the course so good luck to any soldier who has to take it. I very much hope the entire department wakes up and realizes that this is their worst course, but seeing how these problems have persisted for the last 5+ years I highly doubt it.

have seen way worse but honestly just not a good lecturer at all either. first time you'll cover a concept sometimes will be on a quiz where its just a guessing game at that point. lectures are super fast paced so you dont learn anything and theyre also hella boring. doesnt explain anything he just glosses over stuff at best.

He's really nice and funny, he spent an entire lecture basically giving a standup comedy session which was great. He is also really caring and offers students many ways to succeed.
As a lecturer, he is not the best. Hard to follow/understand at times and can rush through slides. Clicker quizzes can also be annoying since lectures are recorded but still cannot be skipped in-person since they are worth 4% of your grade. Projects are interesting but some can be poorly written. Mostly fair exams, though our final had an average of like 68%. 
Cliff gave a lecture one time while Dr. Mamat was out, and he was definitely a better lecturer. Cannot speak about how he is on the administrative/personal side of things, where Dr. Mamat mostly excels in.

Dude explained most of the concepts fairly well but for some topics, he kind of just speedran through the slides. Overall, Anwar was co teaching with cliff so we had the same, quizzes, exams, projects but Anwar had in class clicker quizzes while Cliff had separate lecture quizzes for his students of which 20 percent was dropped for both instructors. Overall, Anwar was much more refreshing compared to 216 with herman and his slides were super helpful for the projects and in addition for some projects he also give some tips or boilerplate code to help students get started with some functions whichw as super helpful. Exams were super fair but make sure to prepare for the final since it was definitely harder than the midterms. I kind of went straight into the exam without studying at all which backfired since it was much harder so I got a 70 which dropped my overall grade from a 93 to an 88.

Not sure what the person below says hes not a good lecturer for, cause he is really great and makes class interesting, way better than Cliff and more engaging. We had Cliff sub in for Anwar which I am just falling a asleep. The powerpoint slides are great and very informative. A lot of projects with short deadlines, but willing to give extensions which are nice. Lecture quizzes makes you pay attention in class. Regular discussion quizzes were hit or miss. Exams are fair. One bad side is some of his lectures are very boring which are mainly the useless ones.

He goes super fast through the content which is very jarring for the first few lectures, so trying to take notes is impossible. But, his slides are very good so there's no need to in my opinion. He has clicker quizzes during lectures to keep people engaged and make attendance mandatory, but they're never too bad and they do help with the content. 330 is the hardest CS class I've taken so far, and it's because all of the content is new. Exams are usually fair but hard, and the project take a very long time to complete. The best thing about Anwar is his passion for teaching. He came back from AWS to teach and I can see why, and I'm glad he did.

Great professor, he gives a lot of useful info during lectures and he's the one who will believe in you. He'll give you a chance, like an extension (it never hurts to ask if you really need it)! and he will help you success in his course

He's a great public speaker I think the first half of the semester he was getting back into teacher but after that he was getting better at lecturing. He does clicker quizzes which to be honest only people who don't want to show up to lecture complain about it's a free 4% of your entire grade he essentially gives people answers to it most of the time and there not hard questions.  As for projects, exams, and quizzes he co taught with Cliff so most of the projects and exams were made by Cliff and his TAs from my understanding. Between Cliff or him Anwar is the better 330 professor provides great slides to study off and is more entertaining/ easier to follow.

Anwar's lectures can be dry and boring at times, but he is a good teacher. You can not ask for a more qualified professor who is an expert in the field for CMSC330. Extremely intelligent and funny at times, but tries to avoid joking around too much as 330 has a lot of content. 330 is hard and the projects took me a long time personally, but you can not go wrong with Anwar. Between Cliff and Anwar, I would lean Anwar's way since he has much more experience in the field.

He is a kind person that really cares about how you're doing. You gotta be quick with your fingers if you wanna be able to answer all the clicker questions. The amount of times I missed a clicker was so many :(

This may be the first semester he teach CMSC430 in 2024 Summer. 
The grading is good but the lecture have some problem. He spent too many time in the easy stuff and some core contents were omitted.

Solid professor, really enjoyed CMSC 330 with him. He's also pretty funny lol. He gives you a lot of material to work from and excellent advice. Clicker quizzes are good, it helps with understanding material in real time.

Not a bad teacher and the course isn't too bad either. It's 5 coding projects, one midterm, and one final project that was easy. There's also gradescope quizzes that aren't hard. Takes a while to grade though, and some of the descriptions for projects weren't that clear since they were written this semester. The midterm was also tough, most people didn't do well.

I really liked the class, Anwar is super fun and engaging even if the content can be kind of boring at times. He doesn't do clicker quizzes anymore and now its lecture quizzes that you can do in groups that are assigned over the weekends (they're basically a free 100). There is also a lot of extra credit, 1% for participating in discussion, and 1% if 90% of students do the course evals at the end of the semester. Projects are also worth almost half of your total grade which is great cause it makes it a lot easier to get a good great. I got a 70 on the first exam and still ended up with an A because of all the stuff I just mentioned. If you're choosing between cliff and anwar it won't really matter too much, both are decent teachers

For the Fall 2024 semester, Dr Mamat and Cliff cotaught using Cliffs resources, course organization, and exam styles. Dr Mamat got rid of the clicker quizzes our semester and stopped recording lectures, which I didn’t mind because I went to almost every lecture. Dr Mamat was funny, but his lectures tended to be disorganized. I found them to be very intuitive when he was typing examples into emacs, but he was flipping through his slides, I felt lost. The projects are hard but they teach you the content very well. The exams are fair. Overall, Dr Mamat is a solid choice for this class.

Anwar is a pretty good professor, he keeps his class fun and engaging and he's a pretty nice guy. He spends most of lecture typing code, which I think was nice but also made it a little difficult for me to take organized and helpful notes that I could actually use later. I would definitely recommend taking notes in a google doc or something on your laptop where you can type so that you can actually keep up with everything he's saying/writing. Projects were fairly difficult but that's CS for ya. Also, exams were actually weighted relatively lightly compared to projects (each midterm was 12% of your grade and there were multiple projects worth 8% of your grade each). 

Overall, I would say that Anwar is a pretty solid choice for 330.

Solid professor. Teaching seemed more oriented to the projects, while the notes from Cliff (co-teaching this class with him) helped more with exams. Sometimes was a bit disorganized when going through slides. Code demos were good. The grading was 40% projects, so very generous in terms of exam weight.

I didn’t have the best experience with Dr. Mamat in CMSC330. While he clearly knows the material well, I found his lectures to be hard to follow and often disorganized. He would jump between code and slides without clearly connecting the concepts, which made it difficult to take notes or understand how everything fit together. It felt like he was more focused on getting through the material than making sure students were following along.

He didn’t use lecture recordings, which made it hard to review anything after class, especially for students who learn better by rewatching. I also thought the code demos, while sometimes helpful, were rushed and lacked thorough explanation.

The projects were challenging, and while they were aligned with course content, there wasn’t much support beyond office hours, which filled up fast. There were also times where project specs felt vague or assumed knowledge we hadn’t really covered in lecture yet. The exams were fair, but since most of the emphasis was on the projects, you didn’t get much feedback on how well you were doing until it was too late to make major improvements.

Overall, I think the class could benefit from clearer organization, better pacing in lectures, and more structured support for the assignments. I wouldn’t recommend taking CMSC330 with Dr. Mamat unless you’re really confident learning independently.

Guy with the review below is straight up lying lol, Anwar recorded and posted every single lecture video, found under Panopto Recordings in ELMS, I am really not sure why he is saying he didn't use lecture recordings.

Dr. Mamat is a great professor. He genuinely cares about his students and wants them to succeed, and he wants to cultivate a classroom environment where students feel comfortable and willing to ask questions. He is very good at tying complex concepts into real-life examples to make them more understandable. His exams were fair, and preparation for them helped me to understand the concepts better.
However, I am not the biggest fan of Dr. Mamat's lecture style, I wish he would follow notes/slides a bit more strictly, as sometimes the concepts are a little difficult to follow when taking notes due to lack of clear structure. I also wish he would relate his lectures to the projects a little more, or at least be clearer with which aspects of the content would be on the projects. Many times it felt like I was learning the most from the projects, which isn't necessarily a bad thing, but still I wish that I was a little more prepared going into the projects.
Still, he records all his lectures so if you ever feel lost you can go back and watch the recording. His class was tough but if you put in the work you will be fine.
My breakdown of his exams/projects:
Exam 1: hardest exam, average was a 67
Exam 2: harder content, but written to be easier than exam 1, average was a 78
Projects 1-2 weren't too bad. Projects 3 and 4 start to get more difficult. Project 5 was definitely the hardest. Project 6 was a breeze (intro to Rust). Project 7 TBD.

Anwar was great, his lectures mostly involved typing coding examples to show how they worked which was the best way for me to learn the material. 
However, sometimes it's difficult to follow him when he either teaches another way or skips a couple of steps in his lectures.
My biggest problem was that I felt really unprepared for most of the projects, especially Project 5, which was a complete nightmare made even more difficult this semester. But Anwar's funny, a very kind professor and genuinely cares about his students succeeding.

Dr. Mamat is a great instructor who genuinely cares about his students. He’s very organized and always willing to help, whether it’s during office hours or after class. He posts lecture recordings, which is super helpful, but I definitely recommend attending both lecture and discussion in person. While you can coast by without going, being there in person really makes a big difference in understanding the material and staying on track.

He also makes an effort to get to know his students, which creates a more welcoming and engaging classroom environment. Overall, this class is well-structured and fair, and Professor Anwar does a great job supporting his students throughout. Highly recommend!
Great prof, hard class. Also i'm convinced that Cliff and the evil cult of TA's are the cause of over half the class failing the first exam and quiz. Anwar nice tho :)

Very good lecturer, recordings and slides posted, very nice person.  Only complaint I could make would be the projects were on the harder side but if you start early you'll be totally fine.  Exams were not bad at all and he has all of the old quizzes and exams on the class page.  Overall I would definitely choose anwar again.

He's good. Knows what he's talking about, very approachable, and genuinely wants to see students succeed. He might not come off this way though if he doesn't think you're truly interested or even paying attention, but I think that's fair. Sometimes his organization does confuse me, especially so in this class where I already a little confused to start (low level coding is my weakness). Oh, and also, he's funny.

For the class, please start projects early and try to understand the concepts because it's very satisfying. I would say this class is hard, but I know a lot of people would think it's easy.

i dont know why there are so many positive reviews for anwar, this guys teaching style is so boring to me. all he does in class is open up emacs and type up random examples all class; like sorry i just spaced out so much during lecture because who wants to watch some guy yap as he types up random notes for an hour and 15 minutes?? i wish he would bring back the slides and actually go over them in class because i have to spend the time in class listening to his lectures that dont make any sense and then spend extra time outside of class going over his slides and the mini quizzes in them since he doesn't. also he would like call on people randomly in class like if he thought certain people weren't paying attention which is like my worst nightmare. anyways it doesn't really matter who you take in this course but i would say to take cliff because hes younger and brings the vibes. he had better exam grades on average last semester but this semester anwars sections had higher grades so 🤷.

if you're trying to choose between anwar and cliff I wont lie there's not much of a difference whatsoever since they co teach. you can go to either lecture regardless of what u register for bc anwar got rid of his clicker quizzes. not sure about cliff but anwar doesn't use slides anymore to teach and personally it wasn't the best way for me to learn. exam 1 was pretty horrible for everyone, mean was around 65% or something but I'm sure they'll change it up for future semesters. the exams were pretty rough and the projects were hard because ocaml is really annoying but overall if you really lock in for the exams you should be ok since they give so much practice material.

Anwar is nice, but I really hate his style of teaching since it’s so disengaging. Literally on the first day of class, bro just opened up Emacs and started coding random stuff, and he doesn’t reference his slides at all—or barely does. It’s so hard to take notes or follow along with what he’s saying when he just codes for 1 hour and 15 minutes straight.

Towards the end of February, I started skipping lecture since he unnecessarily went way too in-depth into the concepts and didn’t teach them in the way they actually show up on the quizzes and exams.

This class is nice though because the projects make up a big portion of your grade, and the exams aren’t worth that much. I literally failed the first two exams and did well on the final, and I’m ending with an A- (that’s telling you how easy it is to do well in this class if you lock in on projects).

Quizzes are each only 2% of your grade, but they add up so try to do well on them. You just need to study 2–3 days in advance and do the practice, and you’ll be fine.

The TAs are way more involved in this course compared to other CS classes, but some of them clearly don’t know anything about the concepts. Also, some are super unhelpful on Piazza, not directly answering questions but posting a link to something else because they're too lazy to answer LMAO. But luckily my discussion TA was super smart, so he was very helpful.

Overall, this class won’t be too bad, but try to study in advance and get ahead if you can to make it easier on yourself.

He's not that great of a lecturer, but he's my favorite professor. He's just the best, but not the best at teaching. He's funny too :). Class in general is easy, the projects take a little while, so I'd to starting at least 2 days before the due date. 

It's annoying to have a project due soon when the autograder does not work right and the readme has 500 typos. This isn't Prof. Mamat's fault, though, I'm pretty sure it's Cliff's fault.

The TA's were actually good unlike CMSC351.

Good professor. Quite literally no difference between him and Cliff. Though, Anwar, being more senior, might be slightly better at teaching. 
The exams for both professors are more or less the same. In fact, it's pretty evident that Cliff and/or the TAs make the exams given the incorporation of gen z humor. Regardless, the exams are fairly easy, and the only way you fail is if you don't actually do the projects (i.e. don't GPT projects). 
Both professors' resources are accessible. In fact, most of the resources can be accessed without being enrolled in the class, just search up UMD CMSC 330.

Great teacher and an interesting class. This class is good for people who like lower level computing concepts and are interested in compilers. Anwar's teaching method lends itself well to the structure of this class, as he has a hands on approach to teaching.

Took this course over the Summer with Anwar.  It's same old Anwar, I'm not a huge fan of the way he teaches, but I also don't typically go to lecture, so it's not something too important to me.  This course in particular tends to be taught identically regardless of professor because they're based off some really amazing notes written by someone whose name I cannot seem to find written down anywhere.  If you're comfortable with assembly from 216, and the concepts from 330, you'll do quite well in this class from the notes alone.  Since it was the Summer, it was nothing but projects and a few easy quizzes.  Even the midterms were just projects.  We weren't able to do the actual final, because Anwar only had classes 3 days a week instead of all 5, meaning we weren't able to cover all the content.  We just did the next project as the final instead, so it was incredibly easy but still weighted disproportionately towards our grade. 

Regardless though, it was an interesting class, and besides having to join the Zoom each day (since he took attendance through Zoom polls) it was pretty easy, even if you're not listening to a word he says.  If you like the way Anwar teaches, or if you're very comfortable with writing in assembly, working with parsing and interpreting, and don't mind learning from the (quite comprehensive) notes, then definitely take this class if it interests you.

Not sure if Anwar is the one who does this, but I hate the structure and organization of this class. The only practice tools are old exams and quizzes which most where in a different curriculum and language entirely. The questions on old quizzes are also significantly different than the quiz I took. There are quizzes randomly assigned Friday close to midnight and due Sunday without any warning. Everything is on only on gradescope and nothing is on elms.

Very nice, wholesome guy who cracks some jokes during lecture. However, easily one of the worst lecturers I've ever had. Any reviews saying that all he does in class is open up emacs and type up random examples in a sleep-inducing kinda way are completely accurate. There doesn't seem to be any coherent order between the topics he covers during lectures, and he absolutely does not follow the corresponding lecture slides. For some topics, he covers them a week or more later than when they appear on the lecture quizzes (which are assigned Friday and due Sunday?). One big issue I have is that Anwar doesn't explain some terms at all. After going to discussion, I realized the topics are very simple and could easily be explained in like 3 seconds.

All this being said, 330 has a ton of old quizzes/exams, notes, and resources to reference. It should still be easy to get a good grade if you put effort into the projects and pay attention in discussion.

Do NOT take Anwar for 433. The projects are not well timed, he is a boring lecturer who just refers to his online notes verbatim to teach, and has assigned projects over break and makes them due the same day as an exam. The one thing that I can say about the class is that it is well structured and that he does provide notes, but other professors who have taught 433 before have done a better job at making notes than he has. Very disappointing to experience this considering the nice reviews I have seen and heard from other classmates.

Took him Spring 2025. First quiz, first exam, and project 5 had harsh distributions, but besides that the class was pretty forgiving. Projects were heavily weighted and time consuming, so I recommend starting early. Overall, no complaints - he's a funny guy who has an odd teaching style (literally just writes code in emacs the whole time and rarely opens his slides), but if you are choosing between him and Cliff, I'd recommend Anwar because his exams seemed to be easier than Cliffs this semester (even though the exams are roughly the same across all sections).

Gave good examples during class and in his notes which helped with assignments in the course, especially projects. Lectures are definitely disorganized at times with him jumping around content. But when he is focused, you are able to learn a lot in class plus he is a funny guy. 

Anwar or Cliff?
Doesn't matter. The course is the same no matter what so just choose the lecture time that works best for your schedule.

Look as a person this guy is great he is funny and has good interaction with his students. However, he may be one of the worst lecturers I have had in terms of learning comprehension.  Most of the slides are not in order to his material and often he transitions into examples that sure are useful but then take up a lot of time.  As a result theory is much less covered which is a large issue for a class based heavily in it

Do NOT take 433, especially with him. It is about program validation, not concurrent programming, and it's a pretty useless class in my opinion. He is a really nice professor, but they added a final exam because he realized everyone is using AI for the projects. His lectures are really boring, and he usually confuses himself and the class because he messes up syntax when copying code from his notes in class. His online notes were way more helpful than his lectures.

Bro just don't take this class. Anwar's a nice person and is pretty accommodating & teaches concepts pretty well, but the material is just kind of ass and projects are confusing. Please don't take this class if you don't like proofs... I honestly have no idea why I even decided to take this one and it was one hell of a ride. There are 6 projects, a midterm, and a final. It's just too much. I thought the final was going to be simple enough but it really wasn't :( Projects build on top of each other so if you don't understand concepts you better fix that earlier in the semester.

Not too bad for a 4xx level class. Had 6 projects, 6 quizzes, one midterm, and one final, plus a few attendance points. He recorded video lectures, but I learned more from reviewing the class notes and actually doing the projects, which were pretty though.

Here is the thing, he really just isn't that good of a lecturer. He can be very disorganized, and he can forget to share vital information unless a student explicitly asks. I think his exams/projects were pretty fair. His projects can be a bit tough, but they certainly help you prepare for the exams so make sure to spend time on them.

The class honestly isn't too difficult content wise. Ocaml coding can be a slightly annoying (especially with lexer, parser, interpreter) and the rust rules may be difficult to understand at first but in the grand scheme of the course, nothing is that hard to pick up. I know many people say Anwar is an entertaining lecturer, but I went to almost every class and struggled to pay attention in every single one. Luckily he started giving out attendance extra credit but as far as what he actually taught, it was awfully boring for me and I found myself doing homework for other classes. I basically taught myself all of the content while studying for the exam or doing the projects. You could honestly chose either Cliff or Anwar for this course but it seems like Anwar was more lenient, especially with his extra credit.

Anwar is pretty good. Him and Cliff coteach so no difference as far as exams, projects, etc. He teaches really well imo when he goes off his slides, which is about half the time. And then the other half he just does random examples on emacs which can sometimes be helpful and other times just makes things confusing. But overall he is pretty solid.

This class is probably the worst out of all the lower level CS classes in its current state being ran by Mamat and Cliff. It is ran very unprofessionally and poorly. Dr. Mamat, despite being a funny guy, was a horrible lecturer. He would complain that less people came to class but his lectures were genuinely so hard to follow. He posts lecture videos though which you can rewatch and there are Cliff's notes, so you can still do a decent amount of learning on your own. The previous exam solutions had a lot of errors which never got fixed, and you needed to check on piazza to see if someone had clarified the solutions were wrong for a specific question, which was annoying. It was also annoying when we randomly started doing rust with 2 weeks left to go in the semester, and the specifications for the final exam were "anything mentioned in any lecture/discussion/quiz/project up until the last day of class."

Overall it's definitely not impossible to do well in this class, it's just ran very poorly which makes it frustrating at times.
He's a good professor and a warm person in general. I would recommend him for CMSC330 since I think he's better at teaching than Cliff.

This class might be the most useless class I have taken at UMD. The class isn't that bad, and the projects were fine. He added a final exam worth 25% (which wasn't too difficult). My only complaint is honestly with myself, as I feel like I wasted my time on this class. He gave 1% EC because 90% of the students completed the course eval, which was a nice thing. He took attendance at least once a week on average on GradeScope (worth 5% of your overall grade), so be aware. He does record lectures, so that's a plus. Just look at his website for 433 online and see if you could benefit from learning the material. In my opinion, it is the most useless class (but well, that just might be me, so...). Otherthan that, if you put the effort, it should be decently easy to get an A. Not like a free A class, but very fair to do well.

He is genuinely a very kind and approachable professor, and I would definitely recommend taking CMSC330 with him. While the course itself is certainly challenging, he creates a welcoming and supportive learning environment that truly makes a difference. He is patient, warm, and always considerate of his students, which helps make a difficult class feel much more manageable and encouraging.

I took this course thinking it was going to be about multithreading and concurrency, which it is not anymore. The course itself is very project heavy, which if you do them all makes it rather easyish to get a good grade. He also made the exams pretty easy, in that he hosted review sessions and really hinted at what was going to be on the exam. 

As a professor I think his teaching style is better than people give him credit for, he is pretty funny and tries to make sure he's teaching efficiently as he can. He has a feedback form where you can send anonymous feedback, and was very open to feedback in OH. However, I think he knows the content in this course (Formal Verification) is not that interesting to most people taking the course, which makes it pretty difficult to teach. It also is very useless if for 99% of people taking the course.

I thought the course content was interesting, and it made me think differently about programming, but yeah the content itself will likely not be useful if you just want to become a SWE. I would say if you like Math and Proofs, but also programming take the course.

This class was extremely boring, not well taught, and not useful. Projects were fine. He seems nice as a professor but I could not for the life of me stay focused while he rambles on just pointing at stuff in the code editor in a dry voice. I wouldn't recommend taking this course.
